Paper.title: Phase Field Simulation for Dendrite Growth in Energy Storage Batteries

Model.name: Phase-field model for dendrite growth in energy storage batteries
Model.description: A phase-field framework for simulating dendrite growth by tracking the transformation from electrolyte phase to electrode phase using phase-field variables, free-energy functionals, and coupled evolution equations. The review emphasizes coupling ion concentration, electric field, temperature, stress, SEI effects, and anisotropy.
Model.evidence: The paper states that the phase-field method constructs a free-energy functional and describes dendritic growth through time-dependent evolution equations, with key influencing factors including ion distribution, electric field, temperature, stress, SEI, and anisotropy.

ModelVariant.name: Guyer electrochemical phase-field model
ModelVariant.description: An early electrochemical phase-field model with simplified electrochemical kinetics, linear ion diffusion assumptions, and Poisson-based charge balance.
ModelVariant.evidence: The review identifies Guyer et al. as the first to construct a phase-field model applied to electrochemical reactions, using simplified control equations for phase field, ion diffusion, and potential balance.

ModelVariant.name: Shibuta KKS-coupled phase-field model
ModelVariant.description: A revised phase-field model coupling Kim-Kim-Suzuki equations, considering interfacial energy and chemical potential, and extending from 1D to 2D.
ModelVariant.evidence: The review states that Shibuta et al. coupled KKS equations into the control equations, yielding a more accurate description of phase field, ion concentration, and electric potential evolution.

ModelVariant.name: Nonlinear nonequilibrium electrodeposition phase-field model
ModelVariant.description: A nonlinear phase-field model for highly nonequilibrium electrochemical systems; its phase-field evolution includes both free-energy and electrochemical-reaction driving forces, and ion diffusion includes ion accumulation and consumption during dendrite growth.
ModelVariant.evidence: The review describes a nonlinear model developed in 2012 for highly nonequilibrium electrochemical systems, broader than earlier linear or semi-linear models.

ModelVariant.name: Chen Butler-Volmer/Nernst-Planck/charge-neutrality phase-field model
ModelVariant.description: A widely used phase-field model integrating a modified Butler-Volmer equation, Nernst-Planck transport, and charge neutrality to solve phase field, electric potential, and ion concentration simultaneously.
ModelVariant.evidence: The review identifies Chen et al.'s 2015 model as widely used, with control equations coupling phase field, overpotential, electric potential, and ion concentration.

ModelVariant.name: Temperature-coupled phase-field model
ModelVariant.description: A phase-field model coupled to heat transfer, first through a simple temperature equation and later through a heat-transfer model including internal heat production and temperature-dependent transport parameters.
ModelVariant.evidence: The review discusses Assadi's early temperature coupling and Yan et al.'s 2018 coupling of Chen's model with heat transfer.

ModelVariant.name: Stress-coupled phase-field model
ModelVariant.origin_paper: Yurkiv et al., 2018; Hong et al.
ModelVariant.description: A phase-field formulation incorporating elastic strain energy and mechanical equilibrium to model stress evolution during dendrite growth, especially in solid-state electrolytes and cracked media.
ModelVariant.evidence: The review explains that elastic strain energy is included in the total free energy and the mechanical equilibrium equation is solved to obtain local stress tensors.

ModelVariant.name: SEI-aware phase-field model
ModelVariant.origin_paper: Mu et al.; Chen et al.; Yurkiv et al.
ModelVariant.description: A family of phase-field models accounting for the solid electrolyte interphase via geometric reconstruction, current correction, or free-energy noise terms; later work begins to incorporate SEI chemistry.
ModelVariant.evidence: The review describes approaches using porous SEI reconstruction, current correction factors, and noise terms, and notes recent progress on incorporating chemical characteristics of the SEI.

ModelVariant.name: Anisotropy-aware phase-field model
ModelVariant.description: A phase-field model with anisotropic interface thickness or surface energy to capture directional growth, branching, and coarsening of dendrites.
ModelVariant.evidence: The review notes that anisotropy is introduced either through angle-dependent interface thickness or the widely adopted surface-energy anisotropy formulation k(theta)=k0[1+delta cos(lambda theta)].

Equation.name: Allen-Cahn evolution equation
Equation.description: Describes the evolution of non-conserved phase-field variables representing phase states.
Equation.evidence: The review presents the Allen-Cahn equation as the evolution law for non-conserved phase-field variables.

Equation.name: Cahn-Hilliard equation
Equation.description: Describes the evolution of conserved concentration variables when no chemical reaction is present.
Equation.evidence: The review presents the Cahn-Hilliard equation for conserved concentration variables ci.

Equation.name: Total free-energy functional
Equation.expression: F = ∫V f dV = ∫V (fchem + felec + felas + ... ) dV
Equation.description: Defines the total free energy as the sum of chemical, electrostatic, elastic, and other contributions.
Equation.evidence: The review defines the total free energy as an integral over energy terms including chemical, electrostatic, and elastic energies.

Equation.name: Guyer phase-field control equation
Equation.expression: Phase-field evolution with interpolation p(ξ), double-well potential g(ξ), gradient energy kξ, concentrations Cj, and electric field term ε'(ξ)(∇φ)^2/2
Equation.description: Early electrochemical phase-field control equation derived from an ideal-solution thermodynamic framework.
Equation.evidence: The review summarizes Guyer et al.'s phase-field control equation and explains the meaning of ξ, p(ξ), g(ξ), kξ, ε(ξ), φ, Cj, μj, Wj, Mj, Vj, and ρ.

Equation.name: Guyer ion diffusion equation
Equation.description: Simple linear kinetic ion-transport equation used in the early electrochemical phase-field model.
Equation.evidence: The review states that Guyer's ion diffusion equation adopts a simple linear kinetic assumption.

Equation.name: Poisson equation for charge balance
Equation.description: Maintains charge/potential balance at the electrode/electrolyte interface.
Equation.evidence: The review notes that the Poisson equation is used in Guyer's model to maintain potential balance.

Equation.name: Shibuta KKS phase-field equation
Equation.description: A KKS-coupled phase-field equation accounting for interfacial energy and chemical potential.
Equation.evidence: The review describes Eq. 7 as part of the Shibuta model, improving the evolution of phase field, ion concentration field, and electric potential field.

Equation.name: Shibuta concentration/potential coupling equation
Equation.description: Couples concentration evolution to diffusion and electric-potential effects in the KKS model.
Equation.evidence: The review presents Eq. 8 as the second control equation in the Shibuta model.

Equation.name: Nonlinear phase-field evolution equation
Equation.description: Includes two driving forces: interfacial free energy and electrochemical reactions.
Equation.evidence: The review states that Eq. 9 incorporates free-energy and electrochemical-reaction driving factors.

Equation.name: Nonlinear lithium-ion diffusion equation
Equation.equation_role: ion concentration evolution with reaction source/sink
Equation.description: Describes ion diffusion while accounting for accumulation and consumption of lithium ions during dendrite growth.
Equation.evidence: The review explains that Eq. 10 includes accumulation and depletion of Li+ due to chemical reactions at the electrode-electrolyte interface.

Equation.name: Chen modified Butler-Volmer phase-field equation
Equation.description: A modified Butler-Volmer-type phase-field equation with gradient-energy and electrochemical-reaction terms.
Equation.evidence: The review identifies Eq. 11 as a modified Butler-Volmer formulation widely used in later work.

Equation.name: Chen Nernst-Planck and charge-neutrality transport equation
Equation.description: Describes cation/anion transport under diffusion and migration and enforces charge neutrality.
Equation.evidence: The review explains that Eq. 12 uses the Nernst-Planck equation together with charge neutrality.

Equation.name: Assadi temperature-field equation
Equation.description: Early temperature-field evolution equation coupled to solute and phase field.
Equation.evidence: The review cites Assadi's 2007 temperature evolution formula.

Equation.name: Yan heat-transfer equation
Equation.equation_role: temperature coupling with internal heat generation
Equation.description: Heat-transfer equation used to couple temperature to phase-field simulations through diffusion and thermal effects.
Equation.evidence: The review presents Eq. 14 as the mainstream temperature-coupling approach.

Equation.name: Elastic strain energy density
Equation.description: Elastic strain energy term incorporated into the total free energy functional.
Equation.evidence: The review states that Yurkiv proposed calculating elastic strain energy and incorporating it into the total free energy.

Equation.name: Mechanical equilibrium equation
Equation.description: Mechanical equilibrium condition used in stress-coupled phase-field models.
Equation.evidence: The review states that Hong et al. solved the mechanical equilibrium equation to obtain local stress tensors.

Equation.name: Constitutive stress relation
Equation.description: Relates local stress tensor to total strain and eigenstrain.
Equation.evidence: The review presents the stress tensor relation in the stress-coupled model.

Equation.name: SEI noise-augmented free-energy functional
Equation.description: Adds a noise term to the free energy to implicitly represent SEI influence on dendrite growth.
Equation.evidence: The review states that a widely adopted SEI approach is to incorporate a noise term into the total free energy functional.

Equation.name: Anisotropic Allen-Cahn form
Equation.description: Allen-Cahn-type evolution equation used with angle-dependent interface thickness.
Equation.evidence: The review attributes this anisotropy treatment to Assadi.

Equation.name: Surface-energy anisotropy function
Equation.description: Predominant formulation for incorporating surface-energy anisotropy into phase-field models.
Equation.evidence: The review states that Eq. 20 remains the predominant approach for anisotropy.

Term.name: chemical free energy
Term.description: Chemical contribution to the total free-energy functional.
Term.evidence: The total free energy includes a chemical energy term fchem.

Term.name: electrostatic energy
Term.description: Electrostatic contribution to the total free-energy functional.
Term.evidence: The total free energy includes an electrostatic energy term felec.

Term.name: elastic strain energy
Term.description: Elastic/mechanical contribution to the total free-energy functional.
Term.evidence: The total free energy includes an elastic energy term felas.

Term.name: SEI noise term
Term.description: Noise-field term used to represent SEI effects in simplified form.
Term.evidence: The review identifies fns as the noise term in the SEI-augmented free energy.

Term.name: diffusion term
Term.description: Species diffusion contribution in transport equations.
Term.evidence: The review repeatedly describes ion diffusion terms in the Cahn-Hilliard, nonlinear, and Nernst-Planck equations.

Term.name: migration term
Term.description: Electromigration contribution in ion transport equations.
Term.evidence: The review decomposes the Chen transport equation into diffusion, migration, and reduction contributions.

Term.name: reaction term
Term.description: Electrochemical-reaction contribution driving deposition and consumption/production of Li species.
Term.evidence: The review states that electrochemical reaction terms directly affect phase evolution and lithium-ion accumulation/consumption.

Term.name: gradient energy term
Term.description: Interfacial gradient contribution controlling diffuse-interface energetics.
Term.evidence: The review notes the gradient term in the free-energy component of the Chen model and earlier variants.

Variable.name: phase-field variable
Variable.physical_meaning: Describes the phase state of the system; tracks transformation from electrolyte phase to electrode phase.
Variable.evidence: The review states that ξα are the basic field variables describing phase states, and that phase transition means transformation from electrolyte to electrode phase.

Variable.name: concentration variable
Variable.physical_meaning: Concentration of component i in a multicomponent system.
Variable.evidence: The review introduces ci as concentration variables related to the chemical free energy.

Variable.name: electric potential
Variable.physical_meaning: Electrostatic potential field in the electrochemical system.
Variable.evidence: The review explicitly defines φ as the electric potential.

Variable.name: charge density
Variable.physical_meaning: Local electric charge density entering the Poisson equation.
Variable.evidence: The review defines ρ in the Poisson equation.

Variable.name: temperature
Variable.physical_meaning: Temperature field affecting transport and morphology.
Variable.evidence: The review introduces temperature-field equations and discusses temperature-dependent dendrite morphologies.

Variable.name: local stress tensor
Variable.physical_meaning: Mechanical stress state during dendrite growth.
Variable.evidence: The review states that stress-coupled models solve for local stress tensors.

Variable.name: total strain tensor
Variable.physical_meaning: Total strain entering the constitutive relation for stress.
Variable.evidence: The review defines εij as the total strain tensor.

Variable.name: eigenstrain tensor
Variable.physical_meaning: Reference/eigen strain entering the constitutive stress relation.
Variable.evidence: The review defines εij0 as the eigen strain.

Variable.name: cation concentration
Variable.evidence: The review defines c+ as the concentration field of positive ions.

Variable.name: anion concentration
Variable.evidence: The review defines c- as the concentration field of negative ions.

Variable.name: lithium atom concentration in anode
Variable.physical_meaning: Concentration of lithium atoms in the anode phase.
Variable.evidence: The review defines cLi as the concentration of lithium atoms in the anode.

Variable.name: overpotential
Variable.physical_meaning: Electrochemical driving force for deposition and dendrite growth.
Variable.evidence: The review identifies overpotential as a key variable and primary driving force for dendrite growth.

Variable.name: interface orientation angle
Variable.physical_meaning: Angle between reference direction and normal vector of the solid/liquid interface used in anisotropy formulations.
Variable.evidence: The review defines θ in the anisotropy formulation.

Parameter.name: interface mobility
Parameter.physical_meaning: Mobility between phase α and phase β in Allen-Cahn evolution.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines Lαβ as the interface mobility between phase α and phase β.

Parameter.name: atomic mobility
Parameter.physical_meaning: Mobility between component i and component j in the Cahn-Hilliard equation.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines Mij as atomic mobility.

Parameter.name: phase-field mobility / dynamic evolution coefficient
Parameter.physical_meaning: Controls the evolution rate of the phase-field variable.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines Mξ as the mobility of the phase field or dynamic evolution coefficient.

Parameter.name: gradient energy coefficient
Parameter.physical_meaning: Controls interface gradient energy in phase-field equations.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines kξ as the gradient energy coefficient of the phase field.

Parameter.name: dielectric constant
Parameter.physical_meaning: Dielectric property entering the Poisson equation.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines ε(ξ) as the dielectric constant.

Parameter.name: double-well barrier height
Parameter.physical_meaning: Energy barrier between different phase states or components.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines Wj and W as barrier-height parameters.

Parameter.name: diffusion coefficient
Parameter.physical_meaning: Transport coefficient for ion diffusion, potentially phase- or temperature-dependent.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines D(ξ) as the diffusion coefficient and later notes temperature dependence of diffusion coefficients.

Parameter.name: Faraday constant
Parameter.physical_meaning: Electrochemical constant entering transport and reaction equations.
Parameter.evidence: The review explicitly defines F as the Faraday constant.

Parameter.name: gas constant
Parameter.physical_meaning: Thermodynamic constant appearing in electrochemical and thermal equations.
Parameter.evidence: The review explicitly defines R as the gas constant.

Parameter.name: molar volume
Parameter.physical_meaning: Molar volume entering KKS and electrochemical formulations.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines Vm as the molar volume.

Parameter.name: interfacial mobility
Parameter.physical_meaning: Interfacial mobility parameter in the nonlinear phase-field model.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines Lσ as interfacial mobility.

Parameter.name: reaction rate constant
Parameter.physical_meaning: Controls electrochemical reaction kinetics in nonlinear and Chen-type models.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines Lη as the reaction rate constant.

Parameter.name: thermal diffusivity
Parameter.physical_meaning: Thermal diffusivity in the temperature equation.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines αth as thermal diffusivity.

Parameter.name: heat capacity
Parameter.physical_meaning: Heat capacity in the temperature equation.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines cp as heat capacity.

Parameter.name: effective thermal conductivity
Parameter.physical_meaning: Thermal conductivity in the Yan heat-transfer equation.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines Ra as effective thermal conductivity.

Parameter.name: internal heat production rate
Parameter.physical_meaning: Internal heat source term in the heat equation.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines Q as internal heat production rate.

Parameter.name: elastic constant tensor
Parameter.physical_meaning: Elastic stiffness tensor used in stress and strain energy expressions.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines Cijkl as the elastic constant.

Parameter.name: surface energy
Parameter.physical_meaning: Base surface-energy parameter in anisotropy formulation.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines k0 as the surface energy.

Parameter.name: anisotropic strength
Parameter.physical_meaning: Strength of anisotropy in the surface-energy function.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines δ as anisotropic strength.

Parameter.name: anisotropy mode
Parameter.physical_meaning: Mode/order of anisotropy in the surface-energy function.
Parameter.evidence: The review defines λ as the mode of anisotropy.

Phenomenon.name: dendrite growth
Phenomenon.description: Growth of metal dendrites on electrode surfaces during battery charging/discharging.
Phenomenon.evidence: The paper is centered on dendrite growth in energy storage batteries.

Phenomenon.name: electrodeposition
Phenomenon.description: Electrochemical deposition of metal ions leading to interface evolution and possible dendrite formation.
Phenomenon.evidence: The review repeatedly discusses metal deposition and electrodeposition in electrochemical systems.

Phenomenon.name: dead lithium formation
Phenomenon.description: Formation of electrically disconnected lithium during plating/stripping, reducing active lithium surface area and capacity.
Phenomenon.evidence: The review identifies dead lithium analysis as a major application of phase-field models.

Phenomenon.name: stress-coupled crack propagation
Phenomenon.description: Interaction between dendrite growth, stress concentration, and crack formation/propagation in solid electrolytes.
Phenomenon.evidence: The review discusses crack propagation, stress concentration at crack tips, and dendrite infiltration in solid electrolytes.

Phenomenon.name: SEI-mediated dendrite regulation
Phenomenon.description: Modification of dendrite growth by the solid electrolyte interphase through morphology, transport, mechanics, and chemistry.
Phenomenon.evidence: The review dedicates a section to SEI influence on dendrite growth.

Phenomenon.name: anisotropic dendritic growth
Phenomenon.description: Directional dendrite evolution governed by anisotropy in surface energy, interface thickness, or transport.
Phenomenon.evidence: The review describes anisotropy as enabling more realistic branching and coarsening behavior.

MaterialSystem.anode_material: Lithium metal (primary focus); also sodium, magnesium, uranium in discussed literature
MaterialSystem.separator_material: Porous separators and structured separators (in discussed applications)
MaterialSystem.description: Review spans energy storage batteries, primarily lithium metal batteries and solid-state lithium batteries, with extension to other metal systems.
MaterialSystem.evidence: The review focuses on lithium dendrites and also notes extensions to sodium, magnesium, and uranium systems.

Geometry.shape: Electrode-electrolyte interface / simulation domains with planar interfaces and structured geometries
Geometry.description: The review discusses early 1D models, extensions to 2D, and structured geometries such as porous electrodes, grooves, separators, and crack-containing solids.
Geometry.evidence: The review notes that Shibuta extended from one-dimensional to two-dimensional scales and later applications include porous and grooved geometries.

Condition.description: Higher applied voltage or overpotential strongly drives dendrite growth and branching.
Condition.evidence: The review states that increasing applied voltage causes morphology to evolve from mossy/needle-like to tree-like with increased branching, and that higher overpotential accelerates growth.

Condition.description: Temperature influences dendrite size and morphology; many studies show higher temperature suppresses dendrites and branching, though some discrepancies with experiments exist.
Condition.evidence: The review summarizes that increasing temperature often reduces dendrite size and branching, while also noting simulation/experiment discrepancies in some studies.

Condition.description: Higher external stress or stronger solid electrolytes generally suppress dendrite growth.
Condition.evidence: The review states that higher mechanical strength of the electrolyte and greater external stress exert a suppressive effect on dendrite growth.

Condition.description: Higher electrolyte ion concentration can reduce dendrite size, subject to saturation and transport constraints.
Condition.evidence: The review notes that increasing electrolyte concentration can significantly reduce dendrite size.

Condition.description: Higher anisotropy generally increases growth rate and favors needle-like/tree-like morphologies.
Condition.evidence: The review states that increased anisotropy enhances growth rate and changes morphology from tip-splitting/mossy to needle-like/tree-like.

Condition.description: Smaller separator pores promote more uniform lithium-ion concentration on the electrode surface.
Condition.evidence: The review states that smaller pore sizes lead to more uniform lithium-ion concentrations and smoother electrodeposition.

Condition.description: Thicker separators lead to more uniform dendrite growth and lower overall growth rates.
Condition.evidence: The review states that thicker separators result in more uniform dendrite growth and reduced overall growth rates.

Result.description: Phase-field models accurately reproduce dynamic interface evolution and can capture the transformation from electrolyte phase to electrode phase during dendrite growth.
Result.evidence: The review states that the phase-field model accurately simulates dynamic changes of the interface and captures phase transformation.

Result.description: Ion concentration, applied electric field, temperature, stress, SEI, and anisotropy are the six key influencing factors in dendrite-growth modeling.
Result.evidence: The review explicitly lists these six factors and discusses their coupling.

Result.description: Higher overpotential accelerates dendrite growth and increases branching; large overpotential is a critical trigger of dendrite formation.
Result.evidence: The review states that dendrite growth rate accelerates and branching becomes more pronounced as overpotential increases.

Result.description: Pulse voltage charging can shorten and narrow dendrites compared with constant-voltage conditions under equivalent deposition capacity and average current rate.
Result.evidence: The review reports that pulsed voltage yields shorter and narrower dendrites than constant voltage.

Result.description: Higher electrolyte concentration can significantly reduce dendrite size.
Result.evidence: The review cites Wang et al. finding that increasing electrolyte concentration significantly reduces dendrite size.

Result.description: Temperature increase often reduces dendrite size and branching; morphology can shift from tip-splitting tree-like to fibrous or spherical forms depending on temperature and temperature gradient.
Result.evidence: The review summarizes these temperature-driven morphology transitions.

Result.description: Higher mechanical strength of electrolytes and greater external stress generally suppress dendrite growth.
Result.evidence: The review repeatedly notes suppressive effects of higher mechanical strength and external stress.

Result.description: Cracks in solid electrolytes alter dendrite morphology, enable dendrite infiltration, and can be exacerbated by internal stress.
Result.evidence: The review summarizes how crack tips, voids, and excessive internal stress affect dendrite growth and penetration.

Result.description: SEI can suppress dendritic development, but simplified SEI representations often neglect chemical characteristics; incorporating SEI chemistry is a key future direction.
Result.evidence: The review states that SEI suppresses dendrites and that future work should incorporate chemical characteristics of the SEI.

Result.description: Increasing anisotropy enhances dendrite growth rates and can change morphology from mossy or tip-splitting forms to needle-like or tree-like structures.
Result.evidence: The review reports these anisotropy-induced morphology changes.

Result.description: Smaller separator pore size and larger separator thickness improve deposition uniformity and help suppress dendrite growth.
Result.evidence: The review summarizes these separator-related structural optimization results.

Result.description: Dead lithium reduces active lithium surface area, increases overpotential/polarization, and leads to capacity loss; it is influenced by discharge voltage, current, stress, temperature, and dendrite morphology.
Result.evidence: The review states these consequences and influencing factors for dead lithium.

Result.description: Tree-like dendrites produce more dead lithium than needle-like dendrites, and lower temperature increases dead-lithium risk by slowing ion diffusion and lengthening dendrites.
Result.evidence: The review explicitly states both findings.

Result.description: The phase-field framework has been extended beyond lithium to sodium, magnesium, and uranium dendrite systems, showing broad adaptability across metals.
Result.evidence: The review states that the model has been extended to sodium, magnesium, and uranium systems.

Result.description: Experimental studies are generally in strong agreement with phase-field simulations for dendritic, needle-like, spherical, and mossy morphologies, but discrepancies remain and require better thermodynamic parameters and matched boundary conditions.
Result.evidence: The review concludes that experiments strongly support the simulations while also noting remaining discrepancies.
